Facts.text: Battle of Manzikert: Seljuq Turks led by sultan Alp Arslan beat and capture Byzantine Emperor Romanos IV Diogenes
Battle of Marchfeld: Rudolf of Habsburg defeats Ottokar II
Delhi Sultan Alauddin Khilji captures Chittorgarh, capital of the Guhila Kingdom
A conspiracy against Piero di Cosimo de Medici in Florence, led by Luca Pitti, is discovered
Pope Paul III names his son Pierluigi Farnese as Duke of Parma
Cambridge Agreement: Massachusetts Bay Company stockholders agree to emigrate
West India Company conquers Sao Paulo de Loanda, Angola
Fronde uprising against Anne of Austria and Chief Minister Cardinal Mazarin in France
Battle of Plymouth: General-at-Sea George Ayscue of the Commonwealth of England attacks a convoy of the Dutch Republic commanded by Vice-Commodore Michiel de Ruyter; Dutch victory
First Lutheran denomination in North America, the Pennsylvania Ministerium, is founded in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
First recorded ascent of Triglav, the highest mountain in Slovenia, by Balthasar Hacquet
The National Constituent Assembly adopts the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen at the beginning of the French Revolution
John Fitch granted US patent for his working steamboat
American inventor Charles Thurber patents a typewriter
W. A. Bartlett is appointed the first US mayor of Yerba Buena, San Francisco
Battle of Rocky Gap, West Virginia (White Sulphur Springs): Colonel George S. Patton's Confederate forces defeat a Union brigade advance
Sixteen Blacks are kidnapped from Gibson County Jail and lynched in Trenton, Tennessee
Netherlands Social-Democratic Worker's party (SDAP) forms
Electric generator at Niagara Falls produces its first power
Armenian Revolutionary Federation assaults Ottoman Bank in Constantinople to draw attention to mass pogroms and massacres of Armenians instigated by Ottoman Sultan Abdul-Hamid II
Phillies walk 17 Dodgers in a game
Australian cricket all-rounder Frank Tarrant scores 145 and takes 13-67 in a county game for Middlesex as they beat Gloucestershire by an innings and 31 runs in a single day
MLB pitcherWalter Johnson's 16-game winning streak ends
Russian army attacks Austrian army in Galicia
German troops overrun Brest-Litovsk, Russia
Philadelphia Athletics' Bullet Joe Bush no-hits Cleveland 5-0
Winchell Smith and Frank Bacon's comedic play "Lightnin'" premieres in New York City
Surrey cricket all-rounder Percy Fender scores 100 in 35 minutes (113 not out) in a county match against Northamptonshire at the County Ground, Northampton
Japanese cruiser Niitaka is driven onto rocks in a storm at Kamchatka, resulting in 284 deaths
CCC camp opens in Cleveland Metroparks Brecksville Reservation
Pumping to build Treasure Island in San Francisco Bay finishes
British leaders and Arabs fight in Palestine
Montreal Maroons are dropped from NHL
Croatia gets autonomous status
First major league baseball telecast on W2XBS: Cincinnati Reds defeat the Brooklyn Dodgers at Ebbets Field, Brooklyn
WWII: Belgium mobilizes
Chad is the first French colony to join the Allies under the administration of Félix Éboué, France's first black colonial governor
RKC soccer team forms in Waalwijk, Netherlands
7,000 Jews are rounded up in Vichy France
Japanese troops land at Milne Bay, New Guinea
Soviet counteroffensive begins in Moscow
Transport #24 departs with French Jews to Nazi Germany
Bulgaria announces withdrawal, and German troops are to be disarmed
US 12th Army Corps crosses the River Seine east of Paris
Japanese diplomats board USS Missouri to receive instructions on Japan's surrender at the end of WWII
Dodgers' Dan Bankhead, MLB's first African-American pitcher, hits a home run in his first MLB at-bat in a 16-3 loss to Pittsburgh at Ebbets Field, Brooklyn, NYC
Dutch professor Jacob Jongbloed demonstrates an artificial heart in Paris
Fluoridation of San Francisco water begins
"Pather Panchali," an Indian film directed by Satyajit Ray and starring Subir Banerjee, Kanu Banerjee, and Karuna Banerjee, is released
KREY TV channel 10 in Montrose, CO (CBS/NBC) begins broadcasting
Yankees announce the purchase of outfielderEnos Slaughterfrom Kansas City
USSR announces successful test of an intercontinental ballistic missile
Official International Hockey Hall of Fame opens in Toronto
Minnesota Twins' Jack Kralick no-hits KC A's 1-0
West Indies beat England 2-1 in the series, becoming the first holders of the Wisden Cricket Trophy
Italian Communist Party selects Luigi Longo as chairman
Baltimore Orioles' Roznovsky and B. Powell are the fourth to hit consecutive pinch home runs
KLOC (now KCSO) Channel 42 in Charlotte, North Carolina (PBS) begins broadcasting
Dean Chance pitches a 2-1 no-hitter, and the Twins sweep Cleveland
Dutch Second Chamber demands US stop bombing North Vietnam
"Hey Jude" single is released by the Beatles in the US (Billboard Song of the Year 1968, Billboard 10th biggest song of all time 2013)
Minister of Home Affairs Robert Porter resigns from the Stormont government (Northern Ireland parliament)
MLB Baltimore Orioles' Don Buford strikes out five times in a game
The New York Giants football team announces they are leaving the Bronx for New Jersey in 1975
Leo Durocherreplaces Harry Walker as manager of Houston Astros
NY Cosmos beat St. Louis Stars 2-1 to win the NASL championship
The USSR performs a nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeastern Kazakhstan
XX Summer Olympic Games open in Munich, West Germany
David Eisenhower writes his final sports column
Ten-year-old Mary Boitano is the first woman to win the 6.8-mile Dipsea Race in Marin County, CA, beating a field of 1,500 runners
The University of Texas at Arlington is the first accredited school to offer belly dancing
Soyuz 15 carries two cosmonauts to space station Salyut 3
Frank Martinus Arion forms the Surinamese Writers Group '77
Ian Duryreleases the single "Sex & Drugs & Rock & Roll"
Cardinal Albino Luciani of Venice becomes Pope John Paul I
Soyuz 31 carries two cosmonauts, including one East German, to space station Salyut 6
John Birges plants a bomb at Harvey's Resort Hotel in Stateline, Nevada
Pete Comita replaces Tom Peterson as bassist of American rock band Cheap Trick
Space Shuttle vehicle moves to Launch Complex 39A for the STS-2 mission
NASA launches the Telesat-F satellite
Floods destroy most of the old town of Bilbao, Spain
Czech Zdenka Silhava sets a world record in the women's discus with a throw of 74.55 m
Tatyana Kazankina of the USSR sets the women's 3,000-meter record (8:22.62) in Leningrad
Baltimore OrioleEddie Murrayknocks in 9 RBIs in a game against the California Angels
Robert Chambers, the "Preppie Killer," murders Jennifer Levin in New York City's Central Park and later claims "rough sex" as the motive
Rosa Motaof Portugal wins the women's Stuttgart marathon in 2:28:38
Paul Molitorgoes 0-for-4, ending his hitting streak at 39 consecutive games

Iranian refugee Mehran Karimi Nasseri arrives at Charles de Gaulle International Airport in Roissy-en-France, France, and stays for almost 18 years, inspiring director Steven Spielberg to make the film "The Terminal" about his life
Trumbull, Conn., is the first US team since 1983 to win the Little League World Series
Bo Jacksonhits the fourth of four consecutive home runs
Two murdered college students are found in Gainesville, Florida
Royal Brett Saberhagen no-hits White Sox 7-0
Hurricane Andrew makes landfall in Louisiana as a Category 3 storm after causing deaths and severe damage in Florida
Beni-Ali massacre in Algeria; 60-100 people are killed
Michael Johnson breaks the 400 m world record with a time of 43.18 seconds
Australia clinches its first Tri Nations Rugby Series with a 19-18 win over South Africa in Durban; Wallabies center Stirling Mortlock lands four penalties and a conversion
Earth Summit 2002 begins in Johannesburg, South Africa
The Columbia Accident Investigation Board releases its final report on the Space Shuttle Columbia disaster
Félix Sánchez wins the men's 400 m hurdles in 47.63 seconds in Athens, earning the Dominican Republic's first-ever Olympic gold medal
Germany wins its first-ever Olympic women's field hockey gold medal with a 2-1 victory against the Netherlands in Athens
Hamish Carter of New Zealand wins the men's gold medal ahead of teammate Bevan Docherty, while Kate Allen of Austria wins the women's triathlon at the Athens Olympics
New Zealand retains the Tri Nations Rugby Series with a 45-26 win over South Africa in Pretoria; All Blacks flyhalfDan Carterlands 4 penalties and 4 conversions
Russia unilaterally recognizes the independence of the former Georgian breakaway regions of Abkhazia and South Ossetia, prompting the Georgian government to sever diplomatic relations with Russia
The 787 Dreamliner, Boeing's all-new composite airliner, receives certification from the EASA and the FAA
Legionella outbreak in Quebec City, Canada, kills 8 and infects 104
Seventeen villagers in Afghanistan's Kajaki district are beheaded by an unknown organization
Thirty-six people are killed in a bus crash in Yan'an, China
All 25,000 applicants to the University of Liberia fail their university entrance examination
Democratic Republic of Congo's health ministry informs the WHO of an outbreak of Ebola; later confirmed as a different strain to that affecting west Africa
Israel and Hamas accept another ceasefire
WDBJ TV reporter Alison Parker and cameraman Adam Ward are fatally shot live on TV by an ex-colleague in Moneta, Virginia
Half a million people take part in a peace march in Barcelona, Spain, following terrorist attacks
Hurricane Harvey is downgraded to a tropical storm as the National Hurricane Center warns of a “multiday rainfall disaster”
Violent clashes after the conviction of spiritual leader Gurmeet Ram Rahim Singh kill 31 in Panchkula, with 120 admitted to the hospital
Women's Rugby World Cup: New Zealand Black Ferns defeat England 41-32 in the final in Belfast, Northern Ireland
Australian Foreign Minister Julie Bishop resigns after losing the Liberal Party leadership contest
Boxing match between YouTubers KSI andLogan Paulends in a draw in Manchester, England
Danny Boylepulls out of directing the next James Bond film due to "creative differences"
Gamer at a Madden NFL 19 gaming tournament in Jacksonville, Florida, loses, then returns to shoot two dead and wound nine
Moroccan pop singer Saad Lamjarred is arrested in Saint-Tropez, France, on a rape allegation
Pope Francisasks for forgiveness in a speech on child abuse in Dublin during his first official visit to Ireland since 1979
Johnson & Johnson is ordered to pay the state of Oklahoma $572 million in the first trial against an opioid manufacturer
Leonardo DiCaprio's environmental organization, Earth Alliance, donates $5 million to fight fires in the Amazon
Meeting of the G7 agrees on a $20 million aid package to fight fires in the Amazon in Biarritz
At least 100 people are killed in flash floods in the city of Charikar, Afghanistan, with 500 houses destroyed
Dutch author Marieke Lucas Rijneveld (29) is the youngest to win the International Booker Prize for her debut novel "The Discomfort of Evening"
Two bomb blasts from suicide bombers at Kabul Airport, Afghanistan, kill at least 60 civilians and 13 US soldiers amid international efforts to evacuate citizens from the country
The longest alligator ever recorded in Mississippi at 14 ft 3 in (4.3 m) is captured in the Sunflower River, weighing 802.5 pounds (364 kilograms)
Zimbabwe's PresidentEmmerson Mnangagwa, known as "The Crocodile," is elected to a second term amid claims of vote-rigging

Boston Red Sox catcher Danny Jansen becomes the first MLB player to play for both teams in the same game; the game against the Toronto Blue Jays on June 26 is suspended due to weather, and he is traded prior to the resumption
Grand jury in Washington, D.C. declines approval of criminal indictment for felony charges sought by federal prosecutors against Sean Dunn for throwing a sandwich at a law enforcement agent
Battle of Crécy:Edward III's English longbows defeatPhilip VI's army south of Calais in northern France; cannons are used for the first time in battle
Michelangelois commissioned to carve the Pietà sculpture by French Cardinal Jean de Bilhères
English astronomerEdmond Halleyfirst observes the comet named after him
Charles Perrault's poem "The Marquise of Salusses or the Patience of Griselidis" is read aloud at the French Academy and later attached to his "Tales of Mother Goose" (1697)
Felix Mendelssohn's oratorio "Elijah" premieres at the Birmingham Festival in England
The first free kindergarten in the United States is started bySusan Blowin Carondelet, a suburb of St. Louis, Missouri
Harry Houdiniescapes from chains underwater in 57 seconds at Aquatic Park in San Francisco, California
Battle ofTannenbergbegins (WWI): 8th German army defeats Russian Second army
Chicago Cubs'Hack Wilsonhits his 44th home run and breaksChuck Klein's NL record
General Franco's Nationalist troops conquer Santander during the Spanish Civil War

German ambassador Count von Zech-Burkersroda pledges to the Dutch Minister of Foreign Affairs andQueen Wilhelminathat Germany will respect the integrity and territory of the Netherlands, as long as the Netherlands remains neutral in any European conflict
The film "An American in Paris," with music byGeorge Gershwin, directed by Vincente Minnelli, and starringGene Kellyand Leslie Caron, premieres in London (Academy Award for Best Picture, 1952)
US President Lyndon Baines Johnson is nominated to run at the Democratic Convention in Atlantic City, New Jersey
The Beatles,Mick Jagger, Marianne Faithfull, and Donovan meetMaharishi Mahesh Yogiat a retreat in Bangor, Wales
Bobby Orrsigns a five-year contract with the Boston Bruins worth one million dollars, the first million-dollar contract in NHL history
DutchQueen Julianaand Prince Bernhard visit Indonesia, the first Dutch royal visit since Indonesia gained independence from the Netherlands
Ernest Shonekan is appointed interim president of Nigeria by GeneralIbrahim Babangida
"7th Heaven," starringJessica Biel, debuts on Warner Bros.
US PresidentBill Clintonsigns welfare reform into law, representing a major shift in welfare policy
An American 1-2-3 in the 200 m medals at the Athens Olympics; Shawn Crawford wins gold in 19.79 ahead of Bernard Williams andJustin Gatlin
Lindsay Tarpley andAbby Wambachscore as the US beat Brazil 2-1, maintaining an undefeated record to win the women's football gold medal at the Athens Olympics
US Postal Service christens a Los Angeles facility as "Ray CharlesStation"
David Gilmourperforms with his band and the Polish Baltic Philharmonic Orchestra before a crowd of 50,000 at Gdańsk Shipyard, Poland
The Rolling Stonesclose their two-year, 147-concert "A Bigger Bang" tour at the O2 Arena in London, England; the box office gross of the tour exceeds $558 million, a record at the time
Burger King agrees to purchase Canadian donut chain Tim Hortons for $11.4 billion
Manchester City secures a 2-1 win at Bournemouth to begin an EPL record 18-game winning streak;Raheem Sterlingscores for City but is sent off with two yellow cards in injury time
Archbishop Carlo Maria Viganò, former US Vatican Ambassador, claimsPope Francisknows of and ignores sexual abuse allegations against Cardinal Theodore McCarrick
Emmerson Mnangagwais sworn in as President of Zimbabwe
Indonesian PresidentJoko Widodoproposes moving the country's sinking and over-crowded capital from Jakarta to the island of Borneo (Kalimantan)
The Milwaukee Bucks forfeit their NBA playoff game after the shooting ofJacob Blake, leading to the NBA postponing more games
American pop superstarTaylor Swift(35) and American NFL football player Travis Kelce (35) reveal their marriage engagement in social media post, garnering 1.2 million 'likes' in under 10 minutes
French General Leclerc's 2nd Armored Division andCharles de Gaulleparade down the Champs-Élysées in Paris after the liberation of the city from Nazi occupation
British Motor Corporation introduces the Morris Mini-Minor, designed byAlec Issigonis; it is only 10 feet long yet seats four passengers
Voyager 2 takes photos of Saturn's moon Titan
French government denies knowledge of bombing of Greenpeace flagship the Rainbow Warrior in Auckland, New Zealand
King Magnus of Holstein, Danish prince and King of Livonia (1570-78), born in Copenhagen Castle (d. 1583)
Frederick V, Elector Palentine (1610-23), King of Bohemia (1619-20), defeated at Battle of White Mountain, born in Deinschwang, Palatinate (d. 1632)
Thomas Bullis, English composer, baptized in  Ely, England (d. 1708)
Conrad Michael Schneider, German organist, kapellmeister, and composer, born in Ansbach, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1752)
Willem de Fesch, Dutch violinist and composer (Joseph), born in Alkmaar, North Holland, The Netherlands (d. 1761)
Elisha Williams, American rector of Yale College, born in Hatfield, Massachusetts, (d. 1755)
Karl Kohaut, Austrian composer and lutenist, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1784)
Johann Heinrich Lambert, French-Swiss scientist, mathematician and astronomer, 1st real proof that π is irrational, born in the Republic of Mudhouse (d. 1777)
Jean-Baptiste L. Romé de l'Isle, French mineralogist known for his work on crystallography, born in Gray, France (d. 1790)
Joseph-Michel Montgolfier, French balloonists (invented Montgolfier balloon), born in Annonay, France (d. 1810)
Antoine-Laurent Lavoisier, father of modern chemistry (identified oxygen and hydrogen), born in Paris, France (d. 1794)
François Guichard, French guitarist and composer, born in Le Mans, France (d. 1807)
Johann Ernst Rembt, German organist and composer, born in Suhl, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1810)
William Joseph Behr, German writer and politician arrested for sedition, born in Sulzheim, Duchy of Bavaria (d. 1851)
Aloys Schmitt, German pianist, composer and music theorist (Preparatory Exercises, Op. 16), born in Erlenbach am Main, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1866)
Abbas Mirza, Crown Prince of Qājār dynasty of Iran, introduced Western military techniques, born in Nava, Mazandaran (d. 1833)
Manuel Oribe, Uruguayan politician and nationalist, 2nd President of Uruguay (1835-38), born in Montevideo, Río de la Plata (d. 1857)
Benjamin Grubb Humphreys, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Claiborne County, Mississippi (d. 1882)
Danville Leadbetter, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Leeds, Maine (d. 1866)
Nicaise de Keyser, Belgian painter (Battle of Guilder Tracks), born in Zandvliet, Belgium (d. 1887)
Albert of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha, Prince Consort of British queen Victoria, born at Schloss Rosenau, nr Coburg, Germany (d. 1861)
James Harlan, American politician (Rep-Iowa), US Secretary of Interior (1865-66), born in Clark County, Illinois (d. 1899)
Wilhelm Troszel, Polish operatic bass, composer and voice teacher, born in Warsaw, Congress Poland (d. 1887)
Princess Alexandra of Bavaria, German princess and writer, born at Schloss Johannisburg, Aschaffenburg (d. 1875)
Erik Anthon Valdemar Siboni, Danish organist and composer, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1892)
Charles Jackson Paine, American railroad executive, soldier, and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1916)
Theodore Washington Brevard, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Tuskegee, Alabama (d. 1882)
Heinrich Quincke, German internist and surgeon (introduced lumbar puncture), born in Frankfurt an der Oder (d. 1922)
Georg August Lumbye, Danish composer and conductor (Tivoli Concert Hall Orchestra), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1922)
Charles Richet, French physiologist (Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 1913 for work on anaphylaxis), born in Paris, France (d. 1935)
Arnold Fothergill, English cricket fast bowler (2 Tests, 8 wickets. BB 4/19; Somerset CCC, MCC), born in Newcastle upon Tyne, England (d. 1932)
Herbert Booth, English officer of the Salvation army and son of William and Catherine Booth who resigned from the army, born in Penzance, Cornwall (d. 1926)
Aleksandr Kuprin, Russian author (Pojedinok), born in Narovchat, Penza Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1938) [NS=Sept 7]
Lee De Forest, American inventor (Audion vacuum (radio) tube), born in Council Bluffs, Iowa (d. 1961)
Zona Gale, American novelist and playwright, 1st woman to win Pulitzer Prize for Drama (1921), born in Portage, Wisconsin (d. 1938)
John Buchan, Scottish politician, Governor-General of Canada (1935-40), writer (The Thirty-Nine Steps) and historian, born in Perth, Scotland (d. 1940)
Harriet Ware (Krumbhaar), American concert pianist, composer (Women's Triumphal March; The Rose Is Red; The Varying Shore), and music educator, born in Waupun, Wisconsin (d. 1962)
Joe Jeannette, American boxer (world coloured champion 1909), born in West Hoboken, New Jersey (d. 1958)
Guillaume Apollinaire [Kostrowicki], French poet (Alcools), playwright, and art critic (proponent of 'Cubism' and surrealist work), born in Rome, Italy (d. 1918)
Carl Wilhelm de Vries, Dutch jurist, born in Amsterdam (d. 1967)
James Franck, German-American physicist (Nobel Prize for Physics 1925), born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1964)
Earl Derr Biggers, American author ("Charlie Chan" detective series), born in Warren, Ohio (d. 1933)
Gene Gauntier [Liggett], American silent film screenwriter and actress, born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1966)
Jules Romains, French novelist, playwright and poet (Men of Good Will), born in Saint-Julien-Chapteuil, France (d. 1972)
Luis Abraham Delgadillo, Nicaraguan pianist and composer (Inca Symphony), born in Managua, Nicaragua (d. 1961)
Tommy Andrews, Australian cricketer (NSW & Aussie player of the 1920's), born in Newtown, Australia (d. 1970)
Emmy van Lokhorst, Dutch writer and literary critic, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1970)
Ferdinand Bruckner [Theodor Tagger], Austrian-German playwright (Races), born in Sofia, Bulgaria (d. 1958)
Ivan Mihailov, Bulgarian revolutionary (Internal Macedonian Revolutionary Organization), born in Novo Selo, Ottoman Empire (d. 1990)
Richard Pindle Hammond, American composer, born in Kent, England (d. 1980)
Yoon Boseon, Korean politician, President of South Korea (1960-62), born in Asan, Joseon (d. 1990)
Clem Beauchamp, American film actor, stuntman, and production manager (Tarzan; Dick Tracy; The Adventures of Superman), born in Bloomfield, Iowa (d. 1992)
Peggy Guggenheim, American art patron and collector, born in New York City (d. 1979)
Rufino Tamayo, Mexican painter, born in Oaxaca, Mexico (d. 1991)
Hellmuth Walter, German engineer and inventor (rocket engines; gas turbines; submarine propulsion systems), born in Wedel, Germany (d. 1980)
Chen Yi, Chinese communist military commander, politician and Mayor of Shanghai, born in Lezhi County, Sichuan (d. 1972)
Frankie Genaro, American boxer (World flyweight champion 1928-31; Olympic gold flyweight 1920), born in New York City (d. 1966)
Jan de Quay, Dutch politician (KVP party, Prime Minister of the Netherlands 1959-63), born in 's-Hertogenbosch, Netherlands (d. 1985)
Jimmy Rushing, American jump blues singer, known as "Mr. Five By Five" (Count Basie Orchestra, 1935-48), and pianist, born in Oklahoma City (d. 1972)
Maxwell D. Taylor, American general (commander of WWII 101 Airborne Division) US Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff (1962-64), born in Keytesville, Missouri (d. 1987)
Tullio Campagnolo, Italian racing cyclist and inventor (quick release skewer patent; founder bicycle component company Campagnolo), born in Vicenza, Italy (d. 1983)
Christopher Isherwood, English novelist (Goodbye to Berlin), born in High Lane, Cheshire (d. 1986)
Ceferino Garcia, Filipino boxer (World Middleweight champion 1939-40), born in Naval, Biliran, Philippine Islands (d. 1981)
Lester Lanin, American high society ballroom dance orchestra leader (At the Tiffany Ball; Twistin' in High Society!), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2004)[1][2]
Walter Bruno Henning, German scholar and linguist specializing in Iranian languages, born in Ragnit, East Prussia, German Empire (d. 1967)
Eric Davies, South African cricket fast bowler (5 Tests, 7 wickets; East Province, Transvaal), born in King William's Town, South Africa (d. 1976)
Frank Gasparro, American chief engraver of the US Mint (1965-81), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2001)
Gene Moore, American MLB player, born in Lancaster, Texas (d. 1978)
Jim Davis, American actor (Dallas), born in Edgerton, Missouri (d. 1981)
Cees de Lange, Dutch comedian and conferencier, born in The hague, Netherlands (d. 1974)
Julio Cortázar, Argentine writer (Blow-Up, Underground Game), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1984)
Humphrey Searle, British composer and musicologist (Twentieth Century Counterpoint; The Music of Franz Liszt), born in Oxford, England (d. 1982)
Allan Merson, English historian and communist, born in Monkseaton, England (d. 1995)
Virginia Hill, American organized crime figure (girlfriend of Bugsy Siegel), born in Lipscomb, Alabama (d. 1966)
Jan Clayton, American actress (Ellen Miller-Lassie), born in Tularosa, New Mexico (d. 1983)
William French Smith, American lawyer and US Attorney General (1981-85), born in Wilton, New Hampshire (d. 1990)
Louis Stotijn, Dutch bassoonist, and conductor (Residence Orchestra), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 2013)
Ronny Graham [Stringer], American composer, lyricist, screenwriter (M*A*S*H (TV series), and actor (Chico & the Man; Mel Brooks films), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1999)
Brant Parker, American cartoonist (co-creator of "The Wizard of Id"), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2007)
David Begelman, American film producer who embezzled Columbia Pictures, born in New York City (d. 1995)
Naomi Parker Fraley, American who inspired "Rosie the Riveter" poster, born in Tulsa, Oklahoma (d. 2018)
Irving R. Levine, American journalist (NBC News), born in Pawtucket, Rhode Island (d. 2009)
Jay Pritzker, American entrepreneur (co-founder of Hyatt hotels), philanthropist, founder of the Pritzker Prize for Architecture, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1999)
Lazar Nikolov, Bulgarian composer, born in Burgas, Bulgaria (d. 2005)
Wolfgang Sawallisch, German conductor (Vienna Symphony, 1960-70; Philadelphia Orchestra, 1993-2003), and pianist, born in Munich, Germany (d. 2013)
Alex Kellner, American MLB player, born in Tucson, Arizona (d. 1996)
Alain Peyrefitte, French politician (Minister of Justice, 1977-81; Minister of Information, 1962-66) and writer (The Immobile Empire), born in Najac, France (d. 1999)
Jack Hirshleifer, American economist, educator, and author, born in Brooklyn, NYC (d. 2005)
Sangharakshita [Dennis Lingwood], Buddhist Philosopher, Founder of the Western Buddhist Order, born in London, England (d. 2018)
B. V. [Balkrishna] Doshi, Indian architect (Pritzker Prize, 2018), born in Pune, India (d. 2023)[1]
Sam Massell, American businessman and politician (1st Jewish Mayor of Atlanta. 1970-74), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 2022)
Andrew Porter, British organist, opera director, music critic, and librettist, born in Cape Town, South Africa (d. 2015)
Naïm Kattan, Canadian novelist and essayist, born in Baghdad, Iraq (d. 2021)
Peter Appleyard, British-Canadian jazz vibraphonist, born in Cleethorpes England (d. 2013)
Yvette Vickers [Vedder], American actress (Attack of 50 Foot Woman; Attack of the Giant Leeches), and Playboy playmate (July 1959), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. c. 2010)
Maurice Tempelsman, Belgian-American diamond merchant best known as the companion of the late Jackie Onassis, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 2025)
Joe Solomon, West Indian cricket batsman (27 Tests, 1 x 100, 9 x 50, HS 100no; British Guiana, Berbice), born in Port Mourant, Guyana (d. 2023)
Dane Škerl, Slovene conductor, composer (Rhapsodic Symphony), and teacher, born in Ljubljana, Kingdom of Yugoslavia (d. 2002)
Gordon Greig, British political journalist (Daily Mail), born probably in or near Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1995)[1]
Guy Vander Jagt, American politician (Rep-R-MI, 1966-93), born in Cadillac, Michigan (d. 2007)
Joe Engle, American USAF Major General, test pilot (X-15), and NASA astronaut (STS-2; STS-51-I), born in Chapman, Kansas (d. 2024)[1]
Ida Gotkovsky, French pianist, composer (Poème du feu), and teacher, born in Calais, France
Gordon Clough, English journalist (BBC Radio 4), born in Salford, England (d. 1996)
Cornelis Bastiaan Vaandrager, Dutch poet (Long Live Joop Massaker), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1992)
Francine York, American actress (Slattery's People), born in Aurora, Minnesota (d. 2017)
Tom Heinsohn, American NBA star, coach (Boston Celtics) and broadcaster, born in Jersey City, New Jersey (d. 2020)
Jet Black [Brian Duffy], British punk rock drummer (The Stranglers), born in Ilford, Essex, England (d. 2022)
Marcello Gandini, Italian car designer (created first supercars including Lancia Stratos), born in Turin, Italy (d. 2024)[1]
Fred Milano, American doo-wop singer (Dion & The Belmonts), born in The Bronx, New York City (d. 2012)
Don LaFontaine, American voice actor known as "the King of movie trailers", born in Duluth, Minnesota (d. 2008)
Nik Turner, British jazz-rock fusion saxophonist, flute player, singer, and composer (Hawkwind), born in Oxford, England (d. 2022)
Akiko Wakabayashi, Japanese actress (You Only Live Twice), born in Tokyo, Japan
Barbara Ehrenreich, American activist, journalist and author (Nickel and Dimed: On (Not) Getting By in America), born in Butte, Montana (d. 2022)[1]
Barbet Schroeder, Iranian-Swiss director (Barfly, Single White Female, More), born in Tehran, Iran
Chris Curtis [Crummey], British rock drummer, songwriter and vocalist (The Searchers - "Needles And Pins"), born in Oldham, Lancashire (d. 2005)
John E. Blaha, American USAF/astronaut (STS 29, 33, 43, 58, 79/81), born in San Antonio, Texas
Keith Alison, American session and touring guitarist (Paul Revere and The Raiders, 1968-75), and songwriter, born in Coleman, Texas (d. 2021)
Vic Dana [Samuel Mendola], American singer and dancer ("Red Roses For A Blue Lady"), born in Buffalo, New York
Yevgeniy Zagorulko, Russian high jump coach (Andrey Silnov, Yelena Yelesina, Anna Chicherova), born in Tyrma, Russia (d. 2021)
Ulf Sundelin, Swedish yachtsmen (Olympic gold 5.5m class 1968; World C'ship gold Dragon class 1971, 5.5m class 1986), born in Nacka, Sweden
